ending: The peace talks end with a declaration of war. Ethniu, the Last Titan, bearing the Eye of Balor and backed by King Corb's Fomor, stands before the assembled Accords nations and announces her contempt for their order and her intent: Chicago will be destroyed as her first demonstration, its people made an example to humanity. The conference shatters; the powers must choose overnight between scattering and standing, and the book closes on the eve of battle, flowing directly into Battle Ground. The Thomas crisis is parked, not solved: after his inexplicable attempt to assassinate Etri, Harry and Lara broke him out of svartalf custody - fighting through, among others, Ebenezar McCoy, whose hatred of the White Court turned the intercept into a savage grandfather-against-grandson battle that neither fully understands, since Ebenezar still does not know Thomas is his grandson. Thomas, dying from his injuries, was taken by Harry to Demonreach and placed in the island's crystalline stasis - alive, beyond rescue or execution, with his motive still unexplained and Justine, pregnant, waiting. Harry's ties are fraying everywhere: the White Council moves toward expelling him, Ramirez's friendship has curdled into professional suspicion, the rift with his grandfather is open and bleeding, and Winter's debt has bound him to Lara Raith's interests. Murphy, half-healed, arms herself anyway. The Titan's ultimatum hangs over all of it.
in_short: The Fomor propose peace talks with the supernatural nations, hosted by the svartalves in Chicago, and Mab drafts Harry as Winter's security - while assigning him to repay Winter's two favors owed to Lara Raith. Thomas and Justine are expecting a child; the White Council is moving to expel Harry; and then Thomas, without explanation, attempts to assassinate Etri, king of the svartalves, and is captured and held for a death sentence under Accords law. Harry and Lara mount a covert extraction from the svartalf deeps that goes loud: Ebenezar McCoy - Harry's grandfather, ignorant that Thomas is also his grandson and consumed by hatred of the White Court - intercepts them, and the escape becomes a brutal fight between kin. Harry carries the dying Thomas to Demonreach and locks him in the island's stasis, saving his life at the cost of his freedom, his motive never spoken. Then the talks reveal their true purpose: the Fomor present Ethniu, the Last Titan, bearing the Eye of Balor, who declares she will destroy Chicago as a lesson to the world. The book ends on the eve of the battle, with every alliance Harry has strained to breaking.

recaps.text: Previously in the series: Harry Dresden serves Mab as the Winter Knight and wards Demonreach, the island prison for horrors. He destroyed the Red Court, died and returned, and killed the Nemesis-corrupted Winter Lady Maeve, whose mantle passed to his apprentice Molly Carpenter - now the Winter Lady, a secret her family does not share. On Mab's loan, Harry pulled a heist into the vault of Hades beside Nicodemus Archleone, which cost the Denarian his daughter and his standing; Murphy was maimed when Nicodemus broke the holy sword Fidelacchius - reborn later in the hands of Waldo Butters, the new Knight of Faith. The parasite in Harry's head proved to be Bonnie, a newborn spirit of intellect, his daughter with the departed shadow of Lasciel. Harry finally met his human daughter, Maggie, who lives with the Carpenters, and he and Murphy are together at last. His half-brother Thomas Raith of the White Court - kin known only to a few - loves Justine, long recovered from thralldom. In the wider world, the Fomor have spent years raiding humanity's coasts and snatching practitioners; the White Council mistrusts its Winter Knight more each season; Lara Raith rules the White Court through her puppet father; and Winter owes Lara two favors, a debt Mab does not intend to leave unpaid. Beneath everything, the Outsiders' contagion Nemesis has shown it can wear any face, and the Black Council remains unfound.

The book opens on the rarest thing in Harry's life: something like peace. He trains, spends time with Maggie and Mouse, minds Bonnie the skull-dwelling spirit, and navigates being genuinely together with Murphy, who is rebuilding herself around the permanent damage Nicodemus left her. The quiet breaks twice. First, joy: Thomas and Justine are expecting a child - a near-miracle for a White Court vampire and a former thrall, and Thomas asks Harry, awkwardly and sincerely, to stand as the child's family. Second, business: Warden Commander Carlos Ramirez arrives with word that the Fomor - the sea-power that has been raiding humanity for years - have proposed formal peace talks with the nations of the Unseelie Accords, to be hosted in Chicago by the svartalves. Every major power will attend. With the news comes a warning delivered as friendship: the White Council's patience with Harry is finished, and a motion to expel him is gathering votes - his Winter service, the company he keeps, and the fear he inspires have finally outweighed his record. Mab, naturally, has plans for the conference that include her Knight; and quietly, separately, she informs Harry that Winter's outstanding debt to Lara Raith - two favors - is now his to repay, on demand and without reservation.

The delegations gather and Chicago fills with monsters on their best behavior. Harry works security under Mab's eye at the svartalf embassy - the same elegant deeps where Molly keeps her apartments, and where the conference's hosts, King Etri's people, hold hospitality as sacred law. Ebenezar McCoy arrives with the Council delegation, and grandfather and grandson manage both warmth and friction: Ebenezar presses Harry about the dangerous shape of his life - Winter, the island, the White Court vampires he keeps failing to keep at arm's length - with a hatred of Lara's kind that runs deeper and more personal than politics. Then the inexplicable happens. Thomas Raith attempts to assassinate Etri - an ambush inside the svartalf embassy, against the conference's host, an act of war under the Accords - and fails. The svartalves take him alive, barely: beaten nearly to death, held in their deeps to await a justice that has only one sentence for such an offense. He will not say why he did it. Not to his captors, not to Justine, not to Harry. The brother Harry knows would never have done it - not unprovoked, not against his own child's future - and yet every witness and every fact says he did.

The conference convenes over the crisis, with the Fomor's smiling envoys and every Accords nation watching the svartalves for weakness and each other for advantage. Harry visits Thomas in captivity and finds him wrecked in body and locked shut in mind - alive mostly because svartalf law is exact about process. Justine's desperation is absolute, and Lara Raith's position is impossible in the way that suits her talents: the White Court cannot be seen to condone the attack, and cannot afford to lose Thomas either. Lara names the price of her cooperation - and begins spending the favors Winter owes her - to one end: Thomas is not going to die in the svartalf deeps. Harry, bound by Mab's debt, bound tighter by a kinship he cannot reveal, commits to the one course that betrays the fewest people he loves: get Thomas out. The planning is a conspiracy inside a peace conference - access through connections better left unnamed, timing built around the summit's ceremonies, Lara's abilities and Harry's power fitted together - while Harry stonewalls the people he most hates lying to. Ramirez, sensing the evasions, hardens from friend into investigator; Ebenezar circles the White Court entanglement with growing fury; and Murphy, sidelined and unfooled, chooses to be Harry's accomplice rather than his conscience.

The extraction goes in - and goes loud. Harry and Lara penetrate the svartalf deeps to pull Thomas out of his cell, and the escape becomes a running battle through stone corridors, embassy security, and the conference's outer defenses, burning bridges at every step: whatever mercy the svartalves' process might have allowed is gone, and Harry's part in the affair - deniable in theory - is written in wreckage. The worst of it waits at the waterline. Ebenezar McCoy intercepts the fugitives, and the confrontation Harry has dreaded arrives at full power: the old man, seeing his grandson bleeding for a White Court vampire, unleashes a lifetime of grief and hatred, and Harry - who cannot say the one sentence that might stop it, that Thomas is Ebenezar's grandson too - stands his ground. Grandfather and grandson tear the marina apart around them, a fight neither can win and neither will yield, until the Water Beetle runs for open water. Harry takes the dying Thomas where no svartalf writ, White Council warrant, or vampire court can reach: Demonreach. The island's crystalline stasis closes over his brother - not healed, not free, not executed; suspended. Alive at the price of everything else, his motive still a locked door, while Justine waits and the conference Harry has just betrayed prepares for its final sessions.

The reckoning gathers before the summit's close. Harry limps back into the conference's orbit with his alibis thin and his accounts overdrawn: the svartalves cold with grief and offense, Ramirez now treating him as a suspect wearing a friend's face, the Council's expulsion machinery grinding forward with fresh evidence of exactly the lawlessness its members fear, and Ebenezar and Harry left staring at each other across a battlefield's worth of unsaid things. Mab's verdict on her Knight's conduct is characteristically exact - the debt to Lara is being paid, whatever else burns - and Lara herself banks the outcome with professional satisfaction: Thomas lives, and Harry Dresden is now woven into White Court interests more tightly than ever. Through it all, the actual diplomacy - the Fomor's proposal, the question of what the sea-kingdom really wants after years of raids and abductions - remains strangely, deliberately unresolved; their envoys have spent the conference watching the nations perform their rivalries. The players reconvene for the closing session with every delegation's guard already exhausted by scandal. Which, it will shortly be clear, was the design. The Fomor did not come to negotiate. They came to make an announcement, and they brought someone to deliver it.

At the final session the Fomor unveil their true delegation. King Corb's procession parts for a figure out of the oldest dark of human memory: Ethniu, the Last Titan, an immortal of world-ending power, the Eye of Balor burning in her ruined face. Her address to the assembled nations is not a negotiating position; it is a sentence passed. She holds the Accords' powers in contempt - squabbling heirs of the beings her kind once were - and she declares her intent plainly: Chicago will be destroyed, tonight, as the first demonstration of the Fomor's new order, its millions made an object lesson for a humanity that has forgotten fear. The Eye's power, unleashed even in demonstration, is beyond anything the assembled monarchs can casually answer, and the conference ends not in treaty but in triage: the powers must decide, in hours, whether to abandon the city or stand and fight for it. The book closes on the brink - Mab marshaling Winter, the nations choosing sides, Marcone's forces arming, Murphy strapping her weapons over her braces, and Harry standing between his city and a Titan, with his brother in stasis, his grandfather estranged, his Council preparing to disown him, and the battle for Chicago about to begin. The story continues, the same night, in Battle Ground.
